title: AIAA-1997-3369-886
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 2]
1.  **Equations:**

    *   Old NOx standard: NOx = 40 + 2 * OPR (g/kN calculated over the LTO cycle)
    *   New NOx standard: NOx = 32 + 1.6 * OPR (g/kN calculated over the LTO cycle)

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *   **Figure 5:** Shows LTO NOx values for several old and newly-certified propulsion engines as a function of the sea-level static takeoff (SLTO) overall engine pressure ratio (OPR). The graph presumably shows curves, with NOx plotted on the y-axis and OPR plotted on the x-axis. It compares NOx emissions for different engine cycles and design philosophies.

    *   **Figure 6:** Compares the characteristic values of NOx and CO for all the engines presented in Figure 5. Presumably has NOx and CO (g/kN) on the y-axis, and engine type on the x-axis, but this is an assumption.

    *   **Figure 7:**  Presents the LTO NOx emissions of the CF34 single-annular combustor (SAC), CFM SAC, the CFM dual-annular combustor (DAC) and the GE90 DAC as compared to the 1996 ICAO standard. Shows the NOx emission index as a function of engine pressure ratio. The trends are approximately linear.
        *The CFM SAC, CFM DAC, and GE90 DAC curves have slopes of 3.3 (R^2=1.0), 2.3 (R^2 = 0.95) and 2.9 (R^2 = 0.97) respectively compared to 1.6 allowed in the 1996 ICAO standard.
        *The LTO NOx of both of the CFM and GE90 DACs can be best-fitted with a linear curve having a slope of 3.1 and R^2 = 0.99.

    *   **Figures 8, 9, 10 and 11:** These figures are cited as showing trends for NOX/HC and NOX/CO characteristics. In particular Figures 10 and 11 seem to show NOx versus pressure ratio, HC/NO, and CO/NOx characteristics for CFM56 and GE90.

3.  **Tables:**
    * There are no explicit tables on this page.

4.  **Diagrams or Schematics:**

    *   No explicit diagrams or schematics are provided on this page. Figures 1,2,3, and 4 are referenced – suggesting diagrams or schematics related to combustor designs and engine configurations (CF6, CFM SAC, DAC, and LM6000 DLE respectively) exist in other figures, but are not included here.

title: AIAA-1997-3369-886
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 3]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

    *   The document does not explicitly define any mathematical equations, but it references correlations for NOx, CO, and unburned hydrocarbon emissions. There is an implicit reference to a stoichiometry, represented by the symbol phi (φ), and comparison against a value of 1.08 as it impacts the NOx formation rate expressions in different combustion zones.

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *   The text references "Figures 10, 11, 12, 13, and 14". The description suggests:
        *   **Figure 10:** Compares HC/NOx characteristics of DAC and CFM56-5B SAC combustion systems.
        *   **Figure 11:** Compares CO/NOx characteristics of DAC and SAC combustion systems.
        *   **Figure 12:** Depicts a SAC combustion system, identified as Phase I, taken from the work of Segalman et al.
        *   **Figure 13:** Compares NOx emissions of uncontrolled diffusion flame combustors with DLE combustors using NG fuel. It indicates a reduction from 225 PPM NOx to less than 25 PPM NOx for DLE at 100% rated power.
        *   **Figure 14:** Compares NOx-CO emission characteristics of premix DLE combustion systems.

    **General Trends/Conclusions (inferred):**
    *   DLE systems significantly reduce NOx compared to traditional diffusion flame combustors.
    *   Next-generation DAC engines will have lower NOx, CO, and HC omissions than previous generations, and wet-control technologies.
    *   Axially-staged combustion systems pose design challenges, but their NOx/CO characteristics are comparable.

3.  **Tables:**

    *   There are no tables on this page.

4.  **Diagrams/Schematics:**

    *   The DLE combustion system "as shown in Fig. 4" is described as a multi-stage combustion concept. The text notes features such as enhanced lean-blowout performance, blow-out avoidance controls, and acoustic energy management, suggesting a diagram would illustrate staged airflow and combustion zones. The text indicates the premix DLE combustion system utilizes a five-stage combustion staging logic, inferring a diagram would illustrate the multistage elements.

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

The comparison shows the model performs well at high-power ranges (NOxEI >= 10 g/kg) with an agreement of +/- 10%. However, needs improvements in the low- to intermediate-power operations.
    *   **Figures 16 and 17:** Compare predicted and measured COEI and HCEI (Carbon Monoxide Emission Index and Unburned Hydrocarbon Emission Index, respectively) for four combustors.
        The agreement is good if one focuses on higher values only (values equal to or greater than 1.0). The CO model under-predicts up to 50% at lower values. The hydrocarbon predictions are within ± 30%.
        *Figure reference only, no actual graphs shown*
    *   **Figure 18:**  Shows a comparison between the measured and predicted Sauter Mean Diameter (SMD). The comparison is part of a model validation using data from an airblast nozzle.

    *   **Figure 19** Shows a typical illustration for the lack of agreement between the measured and predicted radial profiles of SMD from a comprehensive airblast injector model.
        *Figure reference only, no actual graphs shown*

title: AIAA-1997-3369-886
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 5]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

    *   Σ Abs (% Error) / n - This formula, presented in the context of Table II, represents the average error, where ∑ Abs (% Error) is the sum of the absolute values of the percentage errors and n is the number of data points.

2.  **Graphs and Charts:**

    *Graph Described: Referenced Figures*

    *   **Figure 20:** Shows a comparison between predicted and measured profiles of the three velocity components 17 mm downstream from the swirl cup exit plane. The text indicates the agreement is only qualitatively good, meaning not highly quantitatively accurate, but showing some key features of the flow.

    *   **Figure 21:** Presents axial velocity profiles along the tube centerline in-line with the dilution orifice. These profiles are used to compare the predictions made by different grid resolutions and modeling approximations (coarse vs. fine grid, castellated vs. exact simulation) related to the dilution jet mixing. The best agreement with experimental data occurred with the coarse-grid castellated approximation and the fine-grid fitted simulations.

    *   **Figure 22:** Depicts average radial profiles of temperature at the burner exit plane. It compares predicted and measured temperatures. Used to calibrate the anchored CCD for different modern turbopropulsion combustors.

    *   **Figure 23:** Shows NOx emissions as a function of sea-level idle to maximum take-off power, and CO and HC emissions at simulated idle conditions (constant inlet pressure, temperature, and air flow rate) as a function of fuel/air ratio. The figure demonstrates pre-test predictions agreement with sector rig data.

3.  **Tables:**

    *   **Table I:** Summarized, industry needs to adopt and refine the most recent turbulent combustion modeling and simulation techniques in the design process
    *   **Table II:**  Lists the average error, calculated as the sum of absolute percentage error divided by the number of data points across the whole power range from maximum to idle. Data shows, for Combustor 5, the average errors for the whole power range are 25%, 69% and 62% for NO, CO and HC, respectively. The table includes an analysis showing that the error in NOx prediction is mostly associated with the low-power range, whereas the errors in CO and HC are very high in the high-power range. A definition for high power range is given as with NOxEI greater than 10.0 g/kg, thus the average error for NO is only 8.0%. The corresponding errors for CO and HC are only 1.5 and 0.4 g/kg, respectively.

4.  **Diagrams or Schematics:**

    *   There are no descriptions of distinct diagrams, although Figures 20-23 likely contain visualized computational domains, flow profiles and similar such sketches related to the combustors.

    *   Description: This diagram illustrates a GE/SNECMA CFM56 engine double annular combustor (DAC). It shows the cross-sectional design. DAC designs are intended to provide increased operational flexibility regarding emissions performance.

title: AIAA-1997-3369-886
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 14]
**1. Mathematical Equations:**

*   There are no mathematical equations present on this page.

**2. Graphs and Charts:**

*   **Fig. 10: NOx/HC design tradeoffs for dual-annular combustors (DAC) as compared with the CFM single annular combustor.**
    *   **Axes:** x-axis is LTO HC (Light-off Total Hydrocarbons) in g/kN, y-axis is LTO NOx (Light-off Total NOx) in g/kN.
    *   **Trends/Data:** Presents data for three combustor configurations: GE90DAC, CFMSAC, and CFMDAC. For each configuration, there's a downward trend: As HC emissions increase, NOx emissions decrease. The GE90DAC combustor generally exhibits the highest NOx and HC emissions among the shown configurations.
    *   **Conclusion:** Shows an inverse relationship between HC and NOx emissions during the light-off phase of combustion, and demonstrates performance differences between different combustor designs.

*   **Fig. 11: NOx/CO design tradeoffs for dual-annular combustors (DAC) as compared with the CFM single annular combustor.**
    *   **Axes:** x-axis is LTO CO (Light-off Total Carbon Monoxide) in g/kN, y-axis is LTO NOx (Light-off Total NOx) in g/kN.
    *   **Trends/Data:** Presents data for three combustor configurations: GE90DAC, CFMSAC, and CFMDAC. For each configuration, there's a downward trend: As CO emissions increase, NOx emissions decrease. Again, GE90DAC appears to have the highest NOx and CO emissions.
    *   **Conclusion:** Demonstrates a similar inverse relationship as Fig. 10, but between CO and NOx emissions, and highlights relative emission performance across different combustor designs.

*   **Fig. 12: NOx/CO design tradeoffs for axially-staged combustor (ASC) as compared with the single annular combustor Phase I from Segalman et al \[5].**
    *   **Axes:** x-axis is Emission Index of CO (gm/kg), y-axis is Emission Index of NOx (gm/kg). Both axes are on a logarithmic scale.
    *   **Trends/Data:** Shows data for Phase I (single annular combustor) and Phase II (ASC). Phase I displays higher CO and lower NOx. Phase II exhibits a steeper tradeoff, with emissions converging with increasing CO
    *   **Conclusion:** Compares emission tradeoffs on the staged combustor versus standard, and shows how NOx emissions in staged combustors can be reduced with increasing CO with different tradeoff characteristics .

*   **Fig. 13: Typical NOx emissions from a nominal 30 pressure ratio aero-derivative industrial engine with natural gas and DF-2.**
    *   **Axes:** x-axis is % Rated Power, y-axis is NOx, PPM (15% O2).
    *   **Trends/Data:** Shows NOx emissions versus engine power level for three cases: DF2 (Diesel Fuel 2) without control, NG (Natural Gas) without control, and Dry Low Emission (presumably using natural gas). No control case curves show NOx increasing with power. The "Dry Low Emission" config shows very low emissions that do not change much with power until rated power increases
    *   **Conclusion:** Illustrates the impact of fuel type and emissions control strategies on NOx emissions across the engine's operating range.

*   **Fig. 14: Typical NOx/CO emissions characteristics from a nominal 30 pressure ratio aero-derivative industrial engine with natural gas.**
    *   **Axes:** x-axis is CO emissions,

*   **Figure 16:**
    *   Axes: x-axis is "EICO MEASURED", y-axis is "EICO PREDICTED". Both axes are on a log scale.

    *   Trends: The data points are scattered around the 1:1 line, with some data points deviating significantly from the line. This suggests some level of agreement between predictions and measurements but with some degree of error in the predictions.

*   **Figure 17:**
    *   Axes: x-axis is "EIHC MEASURED", y-axis is "EIHC PREDICTED". Both axes are on a log scale.
    *   Data: The graph compares predicted versus measured emissions index for HC (EIHC) for four different combustors (COMB 1-4).

    *   Conclusion: The figure presents a comparison between predicted and measured SMD. Deviation from the 1:1 line indicates the accuracy of the models. Atomizer liquid type clearly impacts SMD.

*   **Figure 19:**
    *   Axes: x-axis is "Radial distance, m", y-axis is "Sauter mean diameter, µm".

title: AIAA-1997-3369-886
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 16]
**1. Mathematical Equations:**

There are no explicit mathematical equations on this page. However, the table gives percentages of variable, implying these metrics can be calculated using some equations that are not explicitly shown on this page.

**2. Graphs and Charts:**

*   **Figure 21: Axial Velocity Profiles Along the Centerline**
    *   *Axes:* x-axis is X (m), representing the axial distance along the centerline. The y-axis is Velocity (m/s), representing the axial velocity.
    *   *Trends:* Shows axial velocity profiles with different mesh types: Coarse Cavitated, Coarse Fined, Fine Cavitated, and Fine Fined.
    *   *Key Data Points:* A strong 'reverse flow' region around x=0.07m to x=0.11m.
    *   *Conclusions:* Suggests the effect of dilution orifice simulation and mesh size on centerline velocity. The different mesh resolutions impact the negative velocities at the dump plane region.
*   **Figure 21: Axial Velocity Profiles Along the Centerline (Experimental and Fined)**
    *   *Axes:* x-axis is X (m), representing the axial distance along the centerline. The y-axis is Velocity (m/s), representing the axial velocity.
    *   *Trends:* Shows axial velocity profiles with a "Fire Fined" numerical model solution and experimental data.
    *   *Key Data Points:* Near good agreement between experimental data and the Fired Fined simulation data.
    *   *Conclusions:* The Fire Fined simulation correlates will with the experimental data.
*   **Figure 22: Comparison between predicted and measured radial profile of exit temperature**
    *   *Axes:* x-axis is Tlocal/Tavg, representing the normalized temperature. The y-axis is R/R0, representing the normalized radial position.
    *   *Trends:* Shows the radial profile of exit temperature, comparing measured values (MEAS AV) with predicted average (PRED AVG) and predicted maximum (PRED MAX) values.
    *   *Key Data Points:* The measured average (MEAS AV) falls between the predicted average (PRED AVG) and the predicted maximum (PRED MAX) values.
    *   *Conclusions:* Provides an assessment of the accuracy of the model in predicting the radial exit temperature profile.
*   **Figure 23: Comparison between pre-test predictions and measured emissions characteristics:**
    *   *Top Graph:* Emission Index of CO (EICO g/kg) as a function of FUEL-AIR RATIO. There are the "DATA" measurements plotted, and also the "PRE-TEST PREDICATION" plotted. Measurements are done at 7% IDLE condition. The trends indicate that the EICO decreases strongly with increasing fuel-air ratio.
    *   *Bottom Graph:* Emission Index of HC (EIHC  g/kg) as a function of FUEL-AIR RATIO. There are the "DATA" measurements plotted, and also the "PRE-TEST PREDICATION" plotted. Measurements are done at 7% IDLE condition. The trends indicate that the EIHC decreases strongly with increasing fuel-air ratio.
    *   *Conclusions:* These results provide an assessment of the accuracy of the model in predicting CO and HC emissions as a function of fuel-air ratio.

**3. Tables:**

*   **Table I: Accuracy goals for next-generation gas turbine combustion models:**

    | Variable                                     | Predictive accuracy band |
    | -------------------------------------------- | ------------------------- |
    | NOx                                          | ±5%                      |
    | CO and unburned hydrocarbons                 | ±10%                     |
    | Exhaust SAE Smoke Number

title: AIAA-1997-3369-886
text:
[VISUAL CONTENT - Page 17]
1.  **Mathematical Equations:**

*   Note indicates relative NOx levels: High Power = NOx > 10 EI
*   Low Power = NOx < 10 EI

    *(EI is Emission Index which represents the mass of pollutant emitted per unit mass of fuel consumed.)*

2.  **Tables:**

*   Table II shows emissions/LBO/exit temperature prediction agreement.

    Data are presented for various combustor configurations (Combustor 5, Comb 1-4, Combustor 6, Single Reactor) under different power ranges (All, High, Low).
*   The columns represent prediction agreement percentages for different parameters: EINOX, EICO, EIHC, LBO, RPF, PTF.

    *EINOX: Emission Index for NOx*

    *EICO: Emission Index for CO*

    *EIHC: Emission Index for HC (Hydrocarbons)*

    *LBO: Lean Blowout*

    *RPF & PTF: Not clearly defined, but related to predictability metrics*

*   Key data from Table II:
    *   Combustor 5 shows a high EINOX percentage agreement at "All" Power Range (25%, 69% and 62%).
    *   For Comb 1-4, at the "All" power range EINOX, EICO, EIHC has the highest agreement percentile range (35%, 51%, 59%).
    *   The anchored high power has El (Emission Index) units for EICO and EIHC for some data entries. For example, for Combustor 5- anchored High Power, the prediction agreement for EICO is 1.5EI, while EIHC its 0.4 EI.
    *   There is no LBO data for Combuster 6 - concert since N/A is given.
    *   The table includes data referred to "CRX for Emissions from Danis, Pritchard and Mongia (1996)" for Comb 1-4, Single Reactor combustor. The Single Reactor values show the least amount of agreement values for these parameters (ranging 23-34%).

3.  **Graphs and Charts:**
    *   None.
4.  **Diagrams and Schematics:**
    *   None.
